chapters.title: A hidden driver behind every choice
chapters.text:
Just as every animal with a backbone has a gap in its vision, the part of the brain that processes sight has its own kind of blindness, and it is far more powerful. It is called blindsight. The gap in the eye and the gap in the brain are both useful metaphors for the thoughts and feelings that guide us without our noticing.

Like the gap in the eye, hidden biases usually stay out of reach. Like clinical blindsight, hidden biases can steer us while we remain in the dark about it.

Hidden biases can lead us to judge and treat people in ways that come from unspoken feelings and beliefs about the groups they belong to. It may sound strange that our choices can be guided this way without our awareness, yet science has confirmed the gap is real.

This summary offers a deep look at the hidden bias gap and helps you spot the ones you carry.

chapters.title: How the mind slips into error
chapters.text:
Humans often take in the world around them without noticing that they are doing it. Setting the stage for hidden biases is the idea of mindbugs, or mental shortcuts that shape thought. There are a few kinds.

Visual mindbugs are classic illusions that fool the mind about how things really are. They are deep habits of thought that lead to slips in how we see, recall, reason, and choose.

Memory mindbugs include a slip called false alarm, when we recall things that never happened. Mindbugs can make us remember events that did not occur more clearly than events that did.

Availability and anchoring mindbugs show up when one kind of event comes to mind faster than another, so we assume the first kind happens more often. Easier to picture does not mean more common in the world.

Social mindbugs appear when we fail to see people as individuals. We see them as members of a group. Tragic outcomes from misplaced trust or misplaced distrust bear the mark of choices made on group identity.

Researchers across economics, sociology, and psychology have shown that the group a person belongs to can be the main cause of how they are treated.

Real cases often come down to single moments, a smile, a suspicious look, a loan approved or denied, a stop on the street, a promotion, a layoff, or a call on more medical tests. Each choice is one mind sizing up another, and that is where the slips hide.

Social mindbugs are not limited to race or ethnic group. They come from any group that holds meaning for people, including gender, faith, class, sexuality, ability, and job type.

chapters.title: The colors of lying
chapters.text:
Some forces nudge our answers away from the truth. We are only partly aware of these forces, and they pull in many directions, including a wish to be honest. Lies come in shades, and the shades are colors.

White lies are small untruths meant to spare someone from boredom, burden, or hurt. They are called white because the color stands for purity and innocence.

Gray lies are a touch less innocent. Like white lies, they aim to protect feelings, but the feelings they protect are the speaker's, not the listener's.

Colorless lies are the things we keep from ourselves. They are called colorless because the teller cannot see them. A man who is asked how many cigarettes he smokes each day may answer with a number that is too low, all the while thinking he is being honest. Colorless lies are another name for self-deception.

Red lies can give the teller an edge in survival or mating. They may have deep roots in our past as a species. The best science points to how other animals use tricks to stay alive, like insects that look or act like a poisonous species to keep from being eaten.

Blue lies are the strange cases where we say something we know is false because we feel it is more true than the truth. A regular voter who skipped the last vote might still answer yes when asked if they voted, because, in their own eyes, they are a regular voter.

Our daily lives run on a small layer of untruth, and that layer keeps the gears of social life turning.

Sadly, what works in social life does not work for science. Lies are still lies.

chapters.title: Two systems that shape our choices
chapters.text:
What we like, what we reject, what we build, what we hold back, who we walk toward, who we walk away from, all of it can take shape in different ways. Two mental systems cover most of it.

The reflective system is the conscious part that dares to be different. If a person on this level is supportive of gay people, they can say in good faith that there is nothing wrong with being gay.

The automatic system is more like cultural norms. A person may believe that being gay is fine, but if they grew up in a culture that treats it as a sickness or a sin, their thoughts, feelings, and actions may still bend under that weight. This is why some people feel shame at the idea of being seen as gay.

We know our reflective views well, especially on matters that matter to us.

We can speak about our faith, or the lack of it. We can lay out why one candidate fits us better than another. We can even say something complex, like, even though candidate X would help me, I will vote for Y because Y is better for the country. Sometimes those views show up in what we do.

The automatic system is a different beast. It is a stranger. We feel something, and that feeling often shows up in our actions. The catch is that we cannot always explain those actions, and they sometimes clash with what we meant to do.

Picture a woman who meets a charming and bright man who tells her he is an undertaker. Her reflective side may warm to him. Then, before she knows it, a wave of discomfort rises, the kind tied to working with the dead.

Every day, automatic leanings pull us toward choices we are only partly aware of, and they are hard to explain because they stay out of reach of our conscious motives.

chapters.title: How the mind builds a quick sketch of a person
chapters.text:
The first reaction to the story below is often surprise. That is because an automatic link in the mind fires the word male when you read surgeon. The link that places surgeons as male is part of a stereotype. There is also a likely slip in thought, in this case, missing the fact that the surgeon must be the boy's mother.

A father and his son are in a crash. The father dies at the scene. The son, badly hurt, is rushed to the hospital. In the operating room, the surgeon looks at the boy and says, I cannot operate on this boy. He is my son.

Getting tripped up by this riddle is annoying, even for people who care about equality. How can someone with strong views on equality still slip on this? Because the mind builds a vast mental library that pulls up quick sketches of people on any new meeting. That pull happens without our knowing it.

Stereotypes are often the first thing we reach for when we size up a person.

Older people are forgetful. Koreans are shy. Boston drivers are aggressive. Women are nurturing. Each line links a group to a trait. Are these sketches accurate?

In some way, almost any stereotype is partly right. How could you argue, for instance, that women are not nurturing?

Even when a stereotype is partly right, it is also partly wrong. There are always people in any group who do not fit. There are plenty of outgoing Koreans and plenty of male feminists.

Since every stereotype is part right and part wrong, judging accuracy is hard. Still, we can say that some stereotypes are more solid than others. The link between feminist and female is more solid than the link between Boston driver and aggressive.

It is worth asking how solid a stereotype needs to be before we let it guide us toward a person we have never met.

chapters.title: When a quick sketch does real harm
chapters.text:
Holding a stereotype is often easy to understand, but applying it to one person can be a problem.

A primary care doctor once told a female patient during a checkup that she did not need a routine cholesterol test. A doctor friend told her to push for it. The results came back with levels high enough to start medication right away. That is how risky a quick sketch can be.

The doctor was not thinking that she was not worth the cost, or that he should protect the insurance company. The slip likely came from a mental picture of the typical heart patient, a man, often overweight, often sedentary, and the patient, a fit and active woman, did not fit that picture.

A second example comes in the form of two harmful out group sketches that link Black men to weapons and deny Asian Americans the same standing given to white Americans.

The Black equals harmful sketch. The belief that African Americans are criminals stays in place even though the odds of any one Black man being a criminal are low. A sketch that links a group to violence and crime is grave and shapes the individual, the group, the resources used to fight crime, and the trust that holds a society together. This sketch has played a part in police officers mistaking a Black citizen's wallet, or any other harmless object, for a gun. Sketches like this can drive life or death calls.

The American equals white sketch. Citizenship has a clear legal meaning. Two people, one with European grandparents and one with Chinese grandparents, both born and raised in the United States, are equally American in the eyes of the law. Yet, are they seen that way? Sketches like this can do real mental harm.

A cloud of suspicion follows some people in every society, simply because they belong to a group whose standing is in question.

A short note, in 1970, fewer than ten percent of the musicians in America's major orchestras were women, and women were fewer than twenty percent of new hires.

chapters.title: Working around the hidden gaps
chapters.text:
Mindbugs are usually outsmarted. Still, people often miss the point. Instead of trying to outsmart them, why not handle them the way programmers handle bugs in code, by spotting them and getting rid of them? Sadly, no method has yet been proven for fully removing the mindbugs behind hidden biases.

Mindbugs are stubborn. So, for now, we need workarounds. That brings us back to the outsmarting path.

Outsmarting, rather than removal, has worked in many places, especially with illnesses that have no cure. For malaria, prevention includes bed nets and tools that lower the number of disease carrying mosquitoes. For HIV, prevention includes condoms, and, more cleverly, a range of drugs that fight the virus. We can outsmart mindbugs in a few ways.

Bypassing them. This is also called the no brainer fix. A clear rule that takes a yes or no call out of a decision is a strong way to bypass a mindbug.

Using numbers. Numbers feel objective and free of bias, yet on their own, they do not guarantee freedom from hidden bias or from more open bias.

It is not clear how to get rid of self directed mindbugs, or how to outsmart them. They may shift with exposure to role models who break the pattern.

Mass media is a rich source of counter stereotype role models. Showing healthy and active older people, for instance, may help counter the old equals frail link that fires in the mind.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
It is clear that hidden biases in humans are unwanted byproducts of mental shortcuts that may have helped our species in the past. We have seen how the feelings and beliefs we hold toward others are shaped by what we feel and believe about the groups they belong to.

Mental moves like stereotypes have a large impact on the choices we make and the way we judge people in our daily lives. Age, race, sex, ethnic group, job, and class all shape how we feel about a person. Even when we are not aware of it, the group a person belongs to changes how we see them.

People in our own group get treated differently from people outside it. This is how ideas like white supremacy work, since African Americans and Asian Americans are treated differently, even when they are as American as anyone else by law. Above borders and roots, every person is as human as the next.

Like the authors of Blindspot, we have run into our own hidden gaps. We need to be more aware of how we form opinions and act toward others. It helps everyone to spot hidden biases and work on them. The world would be a safer and better place if we all treated each other fairly and without bias.

A small step to try.

To find your own hidden biases, visit implicit.harvard.edu, where you can take a range of tests in full privacy. The site has logged more than fourteen million tests so far.

If you have read this far, you are already thinking in a new way.
